params.text:
What discrimination looks like

Discrimination means treating someone unfairly because of who they are. The Equality Act 2010 describes several forms it can take. Discrimination is not always deliberate – and it can be carried out by individuals or built into the way a service is run.

In care settings, discrimination can be subtle: assuming an older person cannot make their own decisions, serving food that ignores someone's religious needs, or talking over a person with a learning disability. Recognising these patterns is the first step to preventing them.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Four key forms of discrimination defined in the Equality Act 2010. Read the example, then flip the card to check the term.

dialogs.text: A care home refuses to admit a resident because of their nationality.
dialogs.answer: Direct discrimination – treating someone worse than others because of a protected characteristic.

dialogs.text: An activity is only ever held upstairs, so wheelchair users can never take part.
dialogs.answer: Indirect discrimination – a rule or practice that applies to everyone but unfairly disadvantages a particular group.

dialogs.text: A colleague repeatedly makes jokes about a person's religion, making them feel humiliated.
dialogs.answer: Harassment – unwanted behaviour related to a protected characteristic that violates dignity or creates a hostile environment.

dialogs.text: A worker who reported discrimination is then denied shifts as a punishment.
dialogs.answer: Victimisation – treating someone badly because they have made or supported a complaint about discrimination.

dialogs.text: Staff assume a man with dementia would not enjoy a hand-massage session, so they never offer it.
dialogs.answer: Discrimination by assumption – stereotyping removes choice; always ask the person rather than deciding for them.
